metadata.name: TSP Roth In-Plan Conversion Rules
metadata.description: Rules and mechanics for converting traditional (pre-tax) TSP balances to Roth (after-tax) within the Thrift Savings Plan. Effective January 28, 2026 per FRTIB final rule (91 FR 2583). Includes eligibility, limits, tax treatment, five-year rules, and strategic planning interactions.
sources: 5 CFR Part 1650 Subpart F — Roth In-Plan Conversions
26 U.S.C. § 402A — Optional treatment of elective deferrals as Roth contributions
notes: TSP in-plan Roth conversions are distinct from rollover conversions to external Roth IRAs.
No income limits apply to Roth conversions (unlike direct Roth IRA contributions).
Conversions are irrevocable — no recharacterization allowed since TCJA 2018.
TSP will NOT withhold taxes on conversions. Participant must pay via estimated tax payments.
This file covers rules only. Current contribution limits are in rates-annual.json and tsp-limits.json.

authority: FRTIB final rule published January 15, 2026 (91 FR 2583), amending 5 CFR Part 1650

eligibility.description: Who may request a Roth in-plan conversion within the TSP

eligible_participants.description: Current federal civilian employees and uniformed service members with a traditional TSP balance
Former federal employees or service members who have not withdrawn their entire TSP balance
Spousal beneficiary participants who have inherited a TSP account with a traditional balance

eligibility.requirement: Must have a vested traditional (pre-tax) TSP balance to convert
eligibility.creates_roth_balance: If participant does not have an existing Roth TSP balance, the first conversion creates one

conversion_mechanics.minimum_amount_note: Each conversion request must be at least $500
conversion_mechanics.maximum_per_year_note: Aligns with biweekly pay schedule — allows one conversion per pay period

balance_retention.rule: Participant must generally retain at least $500 in each applicable tax-deferred contribution source after converting

conversion_mechanics.how_to_request: Through My Account section at tsp.gov, as a dollar amount or percentage of eligible balance
conversion_mechanics.mutual_fund_window: Funds in the TSP mutual fund window must be transferred back into core TSP funds before conversion
conversion_mechanics.no_cap_on_total: There is no annual dollar limit on total conversion amount — limited only by available traditional balance
conversion_mechanics.irrevocable_note: Conversions cannot be undone. Recharacterization was eliminated by the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act (P.L. 115-97), effective for tax year 2018 and later.

tax_treatment.general_rule: The converted amount is treated as taxable ordinary income in the year the conversion occurs

withholding.note: TSP does NOT withhold federal or state income taxes on conversions. Participant must pay taxes from personal funds.

estimated_tax_payments.required: Generally yes — to avoid underpayment penalties
estimated_tax_payments.note: Cannot use converted TSP funds to pay the tax — must use external funds (savings, checking, etc.)

tax_exempt_contributions.description: Tax-exempt contributions (e.g., combat zone pay) retain their tax-free status when converted
tax_exempt_contributions.note: Only the earnings on tax-exempt contributions are taxable upon conversion

pro_rata_rule.description: Conversions pull proportionally from all tax-deferred contribution sources
pro_rata_rule.note: If your traditional balance includes both pre-tax and tax-exempt (combat zone) money, the conversion amount is allocated proportionally between taxable and non-taxable portions

state_tax.description: Most states that impose income tax will also tax the conversion amount
state_tax.note: Some states exempt retirement income but may not exempt conversion income — check state-specific rules in state-benefits.json

five_year_rules._comment: Roth TSP has two distinct five-year rules that affect tax-free withdrawal of earnings

participation_rule.description: The five-year participation clock starts January 1 of the year you first made ANY Roth TSP contribution (or first conversion)
participation_rule.note: If you made your first Roth TSP contribution in 2020, the five-year rule was satisfied on January 1, 2025
participation_rule.applies_to: Tax-free withdrawal of EARNINGS (contributions can always be withdrawn tax-free)

conversion_rule.description: Each Roth conversion has its own five-year holding period before the converted principal can be withdrawn penalty-free if under age 59½
conversion_rule.example: A conversion made in 2026 has penalty-free access to converted principal starting January 1, 2031 (for those under 59½)
conversion_rule.note: This rule primarily affects participants under age 59½. Those 59½ or older with the five-year participation rule satisfied can withdraw all Roth funds tax-free and penalty-free.

qualified_distribution.description: A qualified Roth distribution (entirely tax-free and penalty-free) requires BOTH: (1) five-year participation rule met AND (2) participant is age 59½ or older, disabled, or deceased
qualified_distribution.note: Most federal retirees near retirement age who have had Roth TSP for 5+ years will meet both conditions

withdrawal_ordering.description: How Roth TSP withdrawals are sourced

order.tax_treatment: Tax-free if qualified distribution; otherwise taxable + possible 10% penalty

rmd_exemption.description: Roth TSP balances are NOT subject to Required Minimum Distributions for the original participant
rmd_exemption.note: This is a major advantage of Roth TSP (and in-plan conversions) — converted funds grow tax-free indefinitely with no forced distributions. Inherited Roth TSP accounts may still have RMD requirements for non-spouse beneficiaries.

mandatory_roth_catchup._comment: Separate from conversions — this is a contribution rule that took effect January 1, 2026
mandatory_roth_catchup.description: Federal employees age 50+ whose prior-year Medicare wages exceeded the threshold must make all catch-up contributions as Roth
mandatory_roth_catchup.note: Only catch-up contributions are affected. Regular contributions up to $24,500 can still be split between traditional and Roth as the employee chooses.

planning_interactions._comment: Key interactions between Roth conversions and other federal retirement benefits

irmaa_impact.description: Conversion income counts toward Modified Adjusted Gross Income (MAGI), which determines Medicare Part B/D IRMAA surcharges
irmaa_impact.timing: IRMAA is based on MAGI from two years prior. A 2026 conversion affects 2028 IRMAA.
irmaa_impact.reference: See medicare-rates.json for IRMAA bracket thresholds

fers_srs_interaction.description: Conversion income counts as taxable income but NOT as earned income for the FERS Special Retirement Supplement earnings test
fers_srs_interaction.note: TSP conversions, IRA withdrawals, and pension income do not trigger the SRS earnings test. Only wages and self-employment income count.

social_security_taxation.description: Conversion income increases combined income, which can make more Social Security benefits taxable
social_security_taxation.note: Up to 85% of SS benefits can be taxed. Large conversions in a single year can push the combined income formula above the 85% threshold.

aca_subsidy_impact.description: For pre-Medicare retirees using ACA marketplace coverage, conversion income increases MAGI and may reduce premium tax credits
aca_subsidy_impact.note: Relevant for separated employees under age 65 who are not yet Medicare-eligible

ladder_strategy.description: A Roth conversion ladder involves strategic partial conversions over multiple years to manage tax bracket impact
ladder_strategy.optimal_window: Between retirement and age 72-75 (when RMDs begin), when taxable income is typically lowest
ladder_strategy.goal: Fill up lower tax brackets each year with conversions rather than doing one large taxable conversion
ladder_strategy.reference: See federal-tax-brackets.json for current bracket thresholds

planning_interactions.agency_contributions_note: Agency automatic (1%) and matching contributions are ALWAYS deposited as traditional/pre-tax, regardless of the employee's Roth election. These can be converted to Roth via in-plan conversion.
